name: B.S. in Engineering Technology, Technology Education

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: As a teacher-preparation degree the plan sensibly interleaves an engineering-technology breadth (ECET 201 circuits, MET 103/105 CAD, MNET 300 machining, CET 317 computing, ENGR 320 prototyping, ENGR 330 microcontrollers/IoT, ET 450 multidisciplinary capstone, ENGR 425 advanced-manufacturing rotation) with a professional-education sequence (R300 social foundations and child/adolescent psychology -> methods R300 454/455 -> Clinical Practice I and II student teaching). The progression toward licensure is logical, but the technology content is broad-and-shallow (a survey across many areas) and the education courses are all uncoded.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a coherent depth strand within the technology breadth (for example a two-course computing/robotics-for-educators sequence) so the major is not only a survey.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is shallow (deepest chain 4): the technical courses are mostly introductory (MNET 300 <- MET 103; MIT 231 <- a programming course and IT 120) and the entire professional-education half is uncoded with no parsed prerequisites, so the structure is only partially represented. Only 3 placeholder references (GER 300, PHYS 102A/103A) are missing.
dimensions.suggestion: Assign catalog codes and prerequisites to the R300 education-sequence courses so the licensure progression (foundations -> methods -> clinical practice) is visible in the prerequisite structure.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data. For a licensure program this is especially notable, since teaching standards and candidate outcomes are normally central to the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit candidate outcomes mapped to teaching standards (ITEEA Standards for Technological and Engineering Literacy and state licensure competencies).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, delta 0.0) and per-row credits are shown even for the uncoded education courses, including the 9-credit Clinical Practice II student-teaching block, so the technology/education/gen-ed split is arithmetically transparent. Some terms are heavy (Third Year 2nd Semester at 18 credits) and the final term is light at 12.
dimensions.suggestion: Smooth the 18-credit and 12-credit terms and add a summary of the technology-content vs. professional-education vs. gen-ed credit split.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and titles are present throughout, so the plan is readable, but a large share of rows are uncoded 'None' entries (the entire R300 education sequence, technical/free electives) and there is a cryptic '676 B.S. General Engineering' row and a bare 'R300 378 Seminar in Critical Writing' at 1 credit, which read as artifacts.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the uncoded education-course rows and the cryptic '676 B.S. General Engineering' entry so every requirement row has a code, title and credit value.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The technology content is reasonably current for a STEM teacher-prep program: microcontrollers/IoT (ENGR 330), prototyping (ENGR 320), medical-device cybersecurity (MIT 231), advanced manufacturing (ENGR 425) and a multidisciplinary capstone (ET 450). The notable gaps for contemporary technology education are computer-science/coding pedagogy (CS 106 is the only computing course) and educational-robotics/maker methods, both now national K-12 priorities.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a computer-science-for-educators course and an educational-robotics/maker-pedagogy course to match K-12 CS and integrated-STEM mandates.

descriptionQuality.justification: The 20 coded, sampled courses have specific, informative descriptions (MIT 231 on why medical devices are uniquely vulnerable to intrusion; ENGR 330 on microcontrollers and IoT applications; ET 450 on the multidisciplinary capstone). However, the professional-education half of the program (the R300 sequence and student teaching) is entirely uncoded with no descriptions, so roughly half the program's courses have no catalog detail.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites for the technical courses are clear and sensible (MNET 300 <- MET 103; ENGR 320 <- a calculus course), but MIT 231's prerequisite runs into its description text ('An introductory Computer Programming Course: CS 100 or CS 106 and IT 120'), and the education-course prerequisites/co-requisites are absent, leaving the licensure sequence's ordering unspecified.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 106 is the only computing course, yet K-12 computer-science education is now a licensure priority; a CS-for-educators course is needed.
The methods courses (R300 454/455) are generic; a technology-education-specific engineering-design/PBL methods course tied to ITEEA STEL standards is warranted.
ENGR 320/330 give prototyping and IoT skills but not the classroom integration of robotics and maker spaces that technology teachers must lead.

missingTopics.topic: Data and AI literacy for K-12
missingTopics.rationale: No coursework addresses teaching data or AI literacy, a rapidly emerging curriculum standard for technology educators.

missingTopics.topic: Assessment and Universal Design for Learning in technical subjects
missingTopics.rationale: R300 390 covers assessment generally; a technology-specific assessment/UDL component would strengthen classroom readiness.

emergingTrends.trend: K-12 computer science and computational-thinking mandates
emergingTrends.rationale: States are adding CS graduation requirements, so technology teachers increasingly must teach coding, extending CS 106.

emergingTrends.trend: AI literacy and generative AI in the classroom
emergingTrends.rationale: Preparing teachers to use and teach responsible AI is an immediate need across STEM education.

emergingTrends.trend: Integrated STEM and engineering design (ITEEA STEL)
emergingTrends.rationale: Standards-based integrated-STEM and engineering-design teaching is the field's central framework and could anchor the methods sequence.
Hands-on maker and robotics pedagogy is expanding in secondary schools and connects to ENGR 320/330.
The R300 urban-education sequence positions the program to lead on equity-centered technology instruction.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program/candidate learning outcomes mapped to teaching standards (ITEEA STEL and state licensure).
Strengthen computing by adding a CS/coding-pedagogy course beyond CS 106 to meet K-12 computer-science licensure demand.
Add engineering-design and educational-robotics/maker pedagogy tailored to technology education.
Resolve the uncoded R300 education-sequence rows and the cryptic '676 B.S. General Engineering' entry, and fix the MIT 231 prerequisite/description artifact.
Add AI-literacy content for future teachers and smooth the 18-credit and 12-credit terms.
